story:
The sea churned under a bruised sky, waves battering the rocks and flinging spray high into the air. I stood at the cliff’s edge with my arms stretched wide, eyes closed, smiling into the thunder of water and wind. Seabirds shrieked and wheeled along the cliffs. Then came hoofbeats, climbing the path from the village and stopping behind me.

“Don’t do it, Cassandra! Jumping isn’t worth it.  Think about what it would do to Mother.” my sister cried, her voice thin with fear even at a shout.

I lowered my arms but kept my eyes on a slit in the clouds where sunlight poured down like a blessing onto the cove below. The water there lay darker and calmer, the place where Jeremiah and I used to steal our afternoons—swimming, fishing, dreaming. The village told stories about that hollow—maidens forced from the cliff by pirates, skeletons clutching from the bottom, sea creatures waiting to drag down the curious—but I never believed them. I knew that stretch of water, knew the feel of its pull and where the sand shelved beneath it.

“You’re not being sold,” Sandra shouted, swinging down from the mare, skirts drinking mud. “You’re not the first to be married off to better the family. Lord Welling isn’t the worst fate.”

I turned to my sister. I saw my mare Maggie grazing behind her, sweat starting to dry off her sides from the hard ride.  My eyes moved to my sister who had her skirts collected in both of her arms, minus the back that was trailing in the mud. She was everything Mother prized—polished and careful—while I stood in a plain skirt and worn blouse, wanting only to escape all of it: the balls, the tinsel talk, the legacy pressed like a hand between my shoulder blades.

“Lord Welling is not my cup of tea,” I said. “And I won’t be traded to save Mother’s pride.” I turned back to face the ocean. My eyes scanned the water looking for that familiar boat and sail. And that’s when I saw it, coming around the corner of the cliff on the opposite side of my hole. 

I gasped as I felt hands on my shoulders, forcing my body to twist to face my sister. “Listen to me. We’ve already lost so much,” she said, voice breaking. “Since Luis died, Father’s debts are worse. If you refuse, we’re disgraced. We need this.” She was angry, but her eyes showed that she was scared. This softened my attitude a bit.  

“Okay Sandra.” I spoke calmly to her. “I will come home and give the family what they want.” I saw the relief spread over her face as I'd wanted. “Maggie looks tired.  Let's walk her back home.” My sister nodded gathering her dress again before turning towards the horse.

I helped my sister up into the saddle, and smoothed the blaze down Maggie’s nose. With her back to the sea and eyes on the village road, Sandra said, “In the lee of the headland; jeremiah’s in place.” That’s when I heard it: our signal—short, long, short—a horn from the lee of the headland. Jeremiah was in place.

I turned Maggie towards the village then went back to stand next to my sister’s feet.  “You know, I’m surprised Mother and Father chose me for Lord Welling. You are more suited for Lord Welling. You should take the match.” I paused briefly for my sister to potentially register what I was doing. “Goodbye.” Before she could say or do anything, I smacked Maggie’s behind sending her running towards the village, my sister screaming on her back the whole way.  

I ran. I didn’t stop at the edge. To anyone watching from the green above, I dove toward death. The April water knifed cold as I cut in, and the world went black-blue. My hands found the sandy shelf we’d scouted days ago. I put my feet on the sand and pushed off towards the sky swiftly swimming to the top. As soon as I broke the surface a rope landed on my head with a holler laced with laughter, “Casey!”

I grabbed the rope and let myself be pulled to the safety of the boat. He hauled me in and wrapped me in a blanket, anger and relief warring in his eyes. “That wasn’t the plan, Casey. You could’ve scared me to death.”

“I knew I’d be fine,” I said, breathless and grinning, the cliffs falling away as he turned the skiff toward a patch of clear sky. I slid my arms around his waist. “Let’s break tradition together.”

He bent, kissed me once, and said nothing more as we left the dark cove behind.
